speaker: interviewer
text: We're interviewing Lucie Porges. We're in New York City, and this is Tape number 2. We were speaking about your father and being arrested. Do you know what the charge was?

speaker: interviewee
text: What the charge was? I don't know. There were so many people arrested, just being Jewish, and probably because he wasn't born in Vienna. And I think just being Jewish was good enough for the Gestapo to arrest people at this point, maybe because they thought it would be-- he would then really leave. He would be scared enough to leave. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Well, indeed, he was. As a matter of fact, he was so scared that he absolutely insisted that he would not leave without my mother, my sister, and I. He said there's no way, I don't care if we take nothing with us, that I'm going to leave you here. We are going to leave. And I now in retrospect found out that he also wanted to take his parents, his old parents, and they didn't want to leave. They said they were just too old. They couldn't. So we left my grandparents in Vienna. My father's brother had gone to Switzerland. He was gone already at this point to Switzerland. And we just took-- that was in January, 1939. We just took hand luggage and took a train from Vienna to Cologne in Germany, near the Belgium border, where my parents had heard that there was a possibility of paying somebody to take us over the borders through the night without legality, without a visa, to Belgium. My mother's younger sister was already there-- gone-- had gone the same way in Brussels.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we took that train. And we didn't look particularly Jewish. My father had blue eyes, and-- and nobody really-- we trembled whenever we saw an SS or an SR, but they didn't really bother us because we didn't look Jewish.

speaker: interviewee
text: We got to Cologne and we went to a little hotel. And my parents knew that there was a coffee house called Cafe Jacobi, where all the Jews met and where you could, for money or jewelry, get somebody who would take you over the border who knew how to do it. So we were there about a week, and I remember my parents leaving every morning, trying to make these arrangements. My sister and I stayed together. We walked a little bit through this very strange German city that seemed to have only like tobacco stores and fish restaurants. It was very strange. And after about eight days or so, we were told this is it. This is going to be the night. It was cold. It was winter. And I remember Mother buying us some outfits like they wear now, like jogging suits, brown jogging suits, and some capes, rain capes, in case it got very cold. And my sister-- we left at night with about 15 other people. We walked somewhere in a wood.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then, we arrived. And whenever there were-- there were those big-- how do you call that?-- scheinwerfer, lights looking. And whenever a light came, we threw ourselves on the floor, flat, in the mud and whatever. And then the light went again, and we started walking until the next light came.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we arrived somewhere in a house, where people knew that we were coming. They gave us some coffee and things. And we stayed there for a couple of hours until it was like 2:00 or 3:00 in the morning, and we continued walking, walking. And then we came to a spot where cars were waiting for us, which took us into Brussels. I remember it was raining. It was very dark and wet. And we got to Brussels, and we immediately went to where we knew my aunt was, and we stayed there for a day or so-- for a day or two. And then, already, there sprung up a lot of committees in all those places like Brussel, Antwerp, that helped people like us, because this is a country where we were not expected, and we-- we are not allowed to work or anything. So they gave people like us a little bit of money to be able to exist in that time. And we just lived in one room. Suddenly, coming from Vienna, the whole family just lived in one room. We cooked in that room. We washed in the room. We slept in that room. We didn't have a lot of money, but my mother somehow managed to feed us. But I remember that she bought always one-day-old bread because it was cheaper, and all kinds of odd meats because it was cheap. But she managed to feed us. My sister and I went immediately to a school and learned French very, very fast because we started talking to each other. And my parents-- there was another family living on the same floor-- were working during the night sewing Belgian slippers, which still exists. The Boucherie, they're the best slippers in the world. They're very expensive. They are sewn by hand, and I know they're sewn by hand. They were not allowed to work, so they worked at night to make a little extra money. And life was whatever it was. We went to school. And we were there from 1939 'til 1940. In 1940, the war started. The war with-- first, the Germans attacked Czechoslovakia and Poland. And it slowly got again very upsetting, because we knew it's going to reach us. So it was a very unquiet time, not-- very perturbed. And then, finally, the war came. The Maginot Line didn't hold. And again, we tried to escape. Everybody escaped. We took a train from Brussels. We took, again, a little bit of luggage, whatever, and tried to go to Paris, where a brother of my mother had settled. But we took this train, and we never arrived in Paris. The train was bombarded. We stayed in that train for four days. And after stopping here and stopping there, we arrived somewhere in the south of France, in little-- in a little village. It was a station with-- there was one station for three villages. One was His, Mane, and Touille. And we were-- we were taken to Touille, where we went to an old factory and we were put on straw. We were just put up. And there, we stayed for a few weeks. And we even tried-- there was a school, I remember. My sister and I tried to go to the school, but the accent in-- the southern accent in France is so different from the other French that when we had dictation, we could hardly write it because the pronunciation was so different that it was very hard to follow. And the way they had calcul, math, was like 50 years ago. We were way ahead, so we didn't go back to that school, really. It was ridiculous. And we just lived on things that the peasants sold us. We made our own butter. We learned how to make butter. We bought cream. We put it in a bottle, and we were shaking the bottle, and that's how we made our butter. The place was pretty awful. We had mice, so whenever we had some food like vegetables, we used to hang it up in bags on the walls so the mice couldn't eat it during the night. It was a very, very primitive existence. And then they took my father away, the French. They put him in a French concentration camp called Saint-Cyprien. They took all the men, because they decided being that those men were coming from Vienna or Germany, even though being Jews, they were an enemy of the French state, because the war was with Germany. And they just interned them, no matter what. They were Jewish. They were running away anyway, but they put them in those terrible camps. So my father was put in Saint-Cyprien, which was near the Spanish border, a very primitive camp. After a few weeks, a few women decided to try and go there by train and see what's happening with their fathers and husbands. And I was by that time I think 12 years old or 13, and I decided-- we decided that I would go along and see-- my mother stayed with my sister-- and see what could be done. We arrived there, and being that I was a child, they let me go in. And I could see the conditions of this place. It was summer. People were just dressed very scantily in pajama pants or something. Lots of men were sick. They had malaria, cholera, because there were lots of germs there. And they could only eat if they had something where the soup or something could be put in, like tin cans. If you had a tin can, you could have food, or you would have to lend the tin can to somebody else. I mean, the conditions there were absolutely terrible.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we went to the commandant of this camp, and being that I was a child, he liberated my father, which means that we could go back to this village. I remember walking from the camp to the next train station that was Elne in Les Pyrenees. And it took a long time because my father was so weak. It took a long time to walk there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, finally, we took the train. And there were absolutely magnificent peaches all over the place, and I wanted some, so my father bought me peaches. And I ate so many peaches that I got very sick. I remember that. And then we came back to this village. And we were told that we couldn't stay there, and we had to go to a camp, to a French camp, which was in Brens. It was called Brens, and it was in Albi, near Albi, where Toulouse-Lautrec comes from. And on the way, during the night, we were taken to a prison, a real prison, to sleep over. There was no other place, which was also a very odd experience to be in a French prison. From there, we went to that camp that had about 20 barracks, wooden barracks. Each barrack had about maybe 100 people. There were several layers of couchette where you slept. But the families were together, which was extraordinary. You had a blanket, and there was a common place where you ate. And the barrack just had one little oven in the middle that-- to heat the whole barracks.

speaker: interviewee
text: The food was atrocious. It was mostly topinambur, which is some kind of potatoes, and rutabaga, which is the kind of thing they give to their cows to eat, like-- what is that? Navet in French, rutabaga. It's not like carrots. It's-- I don't know how to call it in English. But it's carrot-like, but that kind of a vegetable. But really, the French only gave it to their animals. And that's what we ate. So my mother at this point-- there was a little town right next to it. And you-- you could go out during the day with a permission-- out and come back to the camp. It wasn't a-- how do you call it?-- a concentration camp. It was a camp where they kept those families. So my mother had a wonderful idea. She started-- she was always very good with sewing, et cetera. She crocheted little napperons, little-- very nice little things.

speaker: interviewee
text: We had very little money. But she went to the people, to the bakers, to the charcutiers. And she got food. She exchanged it against food. She explained that we were in that camp, and then she put the food like under her dress, and-- when she went back. So we always had something extra to eat, a little extra bread, a little extra pate, a little-- whatever she got. She-- she was very creative in this sense, so we were not really hungry. And then slowly, in that camp, where we were just for a few months-- and funnily enough, in that camp, there were lots and lots of young people. And we got together every afternoon, the young people, in that room where you ate. And we sang Jewish songs and we danced the hora, and it was wonderful. It was very uplifting. I loved it. I thought it was marvelous. I really enjoyed being with the young people. The parents were not at all happy, but we were. You know, somehow young people always kind of can do it, can get over this. And after a few months of this camp, somehow we found out, my parents found out, that France-- that they are going to send these people to another camp, which was much worse. So again, we escaped. We escaped, and we went live in Lyon, which is a city in the middle of France. And there, again, we lived in terrible conditions in a very small place in a bad part of the city. But-- it was terrible, but on the other hand, there were always people like us, people that came from Vienna, people that had wonderful backgrounds. So we kind of always got together, and we knew-- we were hoping that it was a temporary thing, that it was not getting worse. So you could take it because of that. And we were in Lyon, I think, from 1941 'til 1942. My sister went to school, and I-- at this point, I think was 13 or 14-- I was always interested in art since I was a little girl in Vienna. Whenever I went to see my grandparents on Sunday, they used to have invitations for weddings and things, very beautiful paper. And I was very bored, so I used to draw women with dresses with little dogs. And I used to draw them-- I used to draw the person in front. And the legs were always in profile, like the Egyptians. I didn't know how to do perspective. So that's how I drew, one after the other, ideas, fashion. And when I came to Lyon, I wanted to draw or do something like this. And somebody got me a job in a place that was painting silk scarves by hand. Now, this was a place-- painting silk scarves, you had the silk flat on a long, long table. There were two pieces of wood. The silk was nailed to it and the middle was very flat. And there was just a little bit of white, flat white, indicating the motifs. And you were supposed to paint flowers. We made beautiful-- I still have one of those scarves. We did beautiful scarves, and there were like three people. So one of them did the reds. One of them did the pinks. One of them did the green leaves. And then we went back, and that's how we finished it, one thing after another after it dried. But it was also a terrible place, because it was very cold. And that's the first time in my life I saw rats. It was full of rats. And in order to work there, you kept seeing them. You always went [STOMPING FEET] with your legs just to make a lot of noise so they would disappear. They were pretty awful. Well, I worked there 'til 1942 when we heard that France was going to be annexed, you know. Half of France was Vichy France, and the other part was still libre, free. But it was going to be annexed, and this was in September, 1942.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I mean, this story repeats itself continuously. We packed again. We took a train. And we went towards the Swiss border, to hopefully go into Switzerland. With us was my mother's brother and his wife. They had no children.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we paid somebody again, same story, who took us to a certain place and then wanted us to start running. There was a little house there. And people came out and said, don't go. Don't go. You'll go right into the hands of the Germans. Don't go. Come. And they took us into their house overnight. Some very, very retired, sweet-- I think they were Swiss people. They lived at the border. And they gave us food and told us that-- they just took us out practically of the Germans' hands, because the next day was a Sunday. And they said if you go around noon, we'll show you exactly where to go. And at this point, you start running. You'll be able to go there without the Germans seeing you, which they did, and we just went to this little village. I remember it was noon. People were sitting in front of their houses, resting. We just went over that spot and started running like crazy. And then, the Swiss police came and took us to their commissariat, and called Geneva and decided-- called Geneva back and forth. And being that my sister and I were children, they kept us. They didn't send us back. This was our greatest, greatest luck. And I think this is what saved us from going to any of the concentration camps or being killed in France, that going over to Switzerland.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Swiss were sending people back?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, they were sending people back. I mean, they sent-- my husband tried to do that when he was a boy, he told me afterwards, and they sent him back once. He went to a French camp to [INAUDIBLE]. And then he tried again, and the second time they kept him. He was just like a month or two before 16, and everybody over 16 had to be sent back. People that were as him, they sent back. They kept him. I mean, it was absolutely sheer, sheer luck, and this because my father just decided not to leave alone and wanted us to come with him, whatever would happen. And not many people did that, because they thought that women and children would be safe. I don't know how he did it, but it saved our lives.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's return a moment to Vienna.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember when you left, the date?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. It was January, 1939.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a sense leading up to that point that other Jews were getting out? Was the neighborhood losing some of its-- its Jewish residents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Absolutely. Absolutely. We were just all put together, and lots of people had left. Some were sent away. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: In crossing the border-- actually, you said that-- that you-- you came to the border, and then people helped you across.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know anything about those people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they were mostly people that-- they were quite well paid. They did it for those reason, I would say. They were not Jewish people. The Jews just tried to put you in connection with them. But they were just people that lived at the border and that somehow made a good living with all these people trying to escape. It was a business, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: When your family packed up and moved, did they leave the home to a caretaker?

speaker: interviewee
text: No way. They left-- I mean, already before, right after Kristallnacht, people came into the apartment, and they-- they absolutely scared us to death. My sister and I, they put us in one room. They closed the door. They took my mother in another room. They screamed. And they came back, and they took a lot of things already then. They came back with big bags. They took silver. They took bedding. They took all kinds of-- whatever they wanted, they just took it. That's all. No, we just-- we knew that we couldn't take anything, furniture. Everything was left. We just took a few things, clothing articles mostly. We just knew we had to leave everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: At that point, I believe you weren't in a-- in a Jewish school.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I wasn't in any school anymore. It was just--

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you think-- excuse me.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was not in any school at this point. We were so scared. It was such a horrible atmosphere. We just tried to stay together, because you never knew if the next moment somebody is going to ring a bell. I was scared to-- to sit on a park bench. You were scared whenever you heard the bell ringing or somebody knocking at the door. Even afterwards in Belgium, I didn't dare sitting on a-- on a bench in a park. I mean, it was just in you.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you think your-- your family was identified as Jewish by these people who would come in?

speaker: interviewee
text: They knew it. They knew our addresses. They knew our name. They knew our religion. They knew it.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were registered in Vienna as Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Absolutely. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: In-- in crossing the border into Brussels, what kind of paperwork did you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: We just had a passport. We managed to have a passport that was apatride, which means we were not Austrian. We were just strangers in Austria living there. And it-- it said that we were Jewish, and that was a passport.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever have contact with the authorities in Brussels, or were you simply smuggled in?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were smuggled in. And then we went to the Jewish community there, and they arranged it for the people. They arranged the papers and whatever, because there were quite a few. We weren't the only ones.

speaker: interviewer
text: In-- in Saint-Cyprien, what kind of people were there? Was it mostly Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Where?

speaker: interviewer
text: In Saint-Cyprien.

speaker: interviewee
text: Saint-Cyprien? Mostly Jews, Jewish men, Jewish men from Germany and from Austria, absolutely.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were the conditions there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bad. Bad. A lot of people died. There was no hygiene. There was absolutely nothing. It was the most primitive of places.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have a sense of how large it was?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was very large, yeah. I think it was very large. There was Gurs in one part of France, and there was Saint-Cyprien on the other side. And there were many in between which I don't know. But this is the one where they took my father and most people that had his nationality, I mean. They are not French and came from Germany or Austria.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you remember the date when he was taken there?

speaker: interviewee
text: We left-- I know we left Brussels in May, 1940. We stayed in this little village for about six or eight weeks. May, June-- it must've been in July, July 1940, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] about that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the conversation you had with the commandant-- the nature of that conversation?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I just said that I had come from this little village and that I would like my father to be released. And he did. He released my father. He gave him a paper and released him officially from that camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was it that you were even able to have a-- an audience with this commandant?

speaker: interviewee
text: I know that people were standing there days and days, waiting to see him. We were just queuing up, lots of people. I don't remember if it took a day or two-- for hours and hours to be able to go in there. Wives and sisters and children, waiting to have their husbands or whoever liberated, in front of this little place.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have a sense of-- of what became of the people who weren't able to leave, who weren't able to get out?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, some of them died. And then some of them were sent on to worse camps. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And finally, I suppose, they were sent on to Germany, to Auschwitz or any place like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And arriving in Brens, can you describe the conditions of that camp also?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. It was about 20 barracks in mud, very bare with straw. And as I said, there was-- in the middle of every barrack, there was a little oven where people in the winter huddled together and sometimes put the bread against the oven to like toast it, some old bread, and put everything they had on their backs to be warm, blankets, anything to walk around. It was very muddy, I remember. There was wooden planks to work on-- to walk on. And then you had to walk all the way through to go to that place where you got hot-- some brew in the morning that supposedly was coffee. I don't know what it was. Awful food, really, just a piece of bread and some slop. That's what it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were most of the occupants Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. They were Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: All of them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, as far as I know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And if you had to estimate the size, each barrack was maybe 100 people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah, about.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was-- were the men involved in any kind of activity?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, everybody was involved in helping, for instance, cooking, serving, cleaning, doing things outside, yes, mopping. Everybody was involved in something that just pertained to the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it unusual that your family arrived intact, or were most of the residents there with their families?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mostly, they were with families, I would say, mostly, in similar cases than ours, I would say.

speaker: interviewer
text: What possessions did you have at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: What possessions? We had some clothes, and I would say that we might still have a little bit of jewelry. My mother probably had her rings and bracelet and things like that, some valuables, you know.
